coredata.dc:title: Plasmonic enhancement of intrinsic carbon nanodot emission
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Schmitz, Rachel D.
Karolin, Jan O.
Geddes, Chris D.
coredata.dc:description: In this Letter we characterize both the photophysical and plasmonic enhanced photophysical properties of carbon nanodots produced by a combustion method. In addition, we have found that max entropy analysis of luminescence decay reveals inhomogeneous distribution of radiative lifetimes. Further, while quantum yields of intrinsic carbon nanodots emission are low [<2%], when in close proximity to plasmonic supporting materials, carbon nanodots emission can be enhanced 30 times (30×).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra recorded for neutral carbon nanodots (CNDs) after 3000rpm centrifugal separation. Spectra are shown for CNDs before and after refluxed in water. Also shown is the absorption spectra observed for CNDs when redissolved in dichloromethane. Note that the distinct absorption band centered at 302nm only appears after the soot sample has been refluxed in 5M HNO3 for 12h.
(A) Emission spectra recorded at different excitation wavelengths for neutral CNDs separated at 5000rpm. (B) Contour plot of the 3D emission spectra data shown in (A).

(A) Time-resolved fluorescence intensity decay recorded for neutral CNDs, separated at 10000rpm, and fitted to a 3 exponential decay function, i.e. k =3 in Eq. (1). The excitation wavelength was 474nm and the emission was collected at 530nm. Note that the instrumental response function (irf) also is shown. (B) Maximum entropy lifetime distribution obtained for data presented in (A). For comparison the maximum entropy lifetime distribution is also shown for Perylene, τ =4.3ns.

(A) Emission spectra recorded for CNDs separated at 10000rpm and excited at 482nm when placed on a MEF-coated 96-well plate (SiF). For comparison the corresponding control sample, recorded from an uncoated well, is also shown magnified 31 times. The constructed MEF spectra, i.e. the SiF signal divided by the signal from the control sample, is shown on the right axis. (B) Synchronous spectra recorded for a CNDs on SiF wells. The inserted images shown real photographs of sample in plastic and SiF wells.

head.ce:title: Plasmonic enhancement of intrinsic carbon nanodot emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Fluorescence and Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Maryland, Baltimore County, 701 East Pratt Street, Baltimore, MD 21202, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Fluorescence and Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Maryland, Baltimore County

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this Letter we characterize both the photophysical and plasmonic enhanced photophysical properties of carbon nanodots produced by a combustion method. In addition, we have found that max entropy analysis of luminescence decay reveals inhomogeneous distribution of radiative lifetimes. Further, while quantum yields of intrinsic carbon nanodots emission are low [<2%], when in close proximity to plasmonic supporting materials, carbon nanodots emission can be enhanced 30 times (30×).

ce:sections.ce:para: Carbon nanodots have been studied heavily over the past decade, due to their various synthetic pathways and their photophysical and related structural properties that make them ideal candidates for various fields of study [1–3]. Several synthetic pathways are described in literature, including ablation, combustion, electrochemical, and arc discharge along a few more [1–3]. Our lab has also been interested in these naturally fluorescent nanosized particles for potential uses in labeling and imaging. Carbon nanodots, produced by ablation, were previous investigated by our lab [4]; however, herein we have investigated carbon nanodots synthesized by another method, combustion. The photophysical and plasmonic enhanced photophysical properties for combustion synthesized carbon nanodots have subsequently been explored.

In order to study the plasmonic enhanced properties of carbon nanodots, metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) has been utilized. Metal-enhanced fluorescence technology has been shown to enhance the luminescence of various fluorophores accompanied by reduced lifetimes and subsequently much more pronounced photostability [5–8]. The enhancement mechanisms of MEF are based on both the absorption and emission components that occur in the near-field when a fluorophore comes in close proximity to plasmons of a noble metal nanoparticle. When the fluorophore and plasmons are within a wavelength of light, coupling occurs (inverse mirror dipole) which ultimately provides for enhanced emission, the metal radiating the coupled quanta [5–8]. MEF has been shown to enhance the emission of fluorophores up to several hundred fold [9], and within this Letter we discuss the uses of MEF with combustion synthesized carbon nanodots, with the view point of enhancing their low quantum yield.

Carbon nanodots were synthesized as previously described, with slight modifications in the separation phase [10,11]. Briefly, a wax candle was used and burnt to collect soot, which in turn was refluxed with 5M HNO3 at 100°C for 12h. The mixture was then neutralized (NaOH) and then centrifuged to achieve different fractions of carbon nanodots. Steady-state fluorescence data were recorded on a Horiba FluoroMax 4 and the absorption data collected on Cary 50 Bio UV-Visible Spectrophotometer. The MEF data were recorded on silvered 96-well Plates 9 using 473 and 532nm laser excitation sources, emission collected using an ocean optics HR 2000+ spectrograph. Additional measurements were performed on a Cary Eclipse Varian. Time-resolved fluorescence decays were obtained using the time-correlated single photon counting technique on a FluoroCube system from Horiba. The samples were excited using NanoLeds centered at 311, 351 and 474nm, the emission wavelength was selected with a monochromator, and subsequently the signal was detected on a TBX-04 timing module from Horiba.

The time dependent decay of fluorescence intensity, i(t), following excitation by a short pulse of light, can often be described by a sum of exponential terms, i.e. [12]
(1)
i ( t ) =
∑
i = 1
k
a i
exp
−
t
τ i
where a
i and τ
i indicates the pre-exponential factor and the fluorescence lifetime of component i, respectively. Multiexponential decay kinetics, i.e. k ≠1, can have its origin due to several different mechanisms, e.g. conformational inhomogeneity in the sample, different electronic states within the chromophoric unit, excited state reactions, quenching, or maybe most obvious, due to a mixtures of several different types of fluorophores in the sample. Identifying the exact nature of a multiexponential photophysics can be a daunting task. In many cases it has proven useful to analyze data in terms of a distribution of decay constants, and rewrite Eq. (1) as [13]
(2)
i ( t ) =
∫ 0 ∞
α ( τ )
exp
−
t τ
d τ
where α(τ) reflects the distribution of decay constants. By using the maximum entropy method (MEM) [12], it is possible to determine α(τ). The optimization criteria is then based on maximizing the Shannon–Jaynes entropy function, S, defined by [14]
(3)
S = −
∑
i = 1
N
p i
ln
p i
where
p i
=
a i
∑
i = 1
N
a i
and by minimizing the reduced chi square values, defined by [15]
(4)
χ 2
=
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
W i
[
I
observed
(
t i
) − i (
t i
) ]
2

For single-photon counting data the weighting factor W
i is given by
W i
= 1 /
N i
where N
i is the numbers of counts in channel i. In the present report, time-resolved data were analyzed using standard reconvolution techniques, evaluating χ 2 for different values of k in Eq. (1), by using the software package DAS 6 from Horiba. The maximum entropy method was performed using a sequential quadratic programming method [13–15] based on C-code.

The absorbance spectrum, Figure 1 , shows a broad absorption with a higher optical density in the UV region and tailing off toward 500nm. This general trend was also seen in all fractions of carbon nanodots collected and over the several different experimental trials of the carbon nanodots. These characteristics found in the absorbance spectrum have been similarly reported by our lab and several others [1–4,8,10].

The emission profile of the carbon nanodots is excitation wavelength dependent, as shown in Figure 2 . The emission intensity significantly decreases at longer wavelengths, >500nm. The normalized spectra (Figure 2, bottom) allow one to clearly see the excitation wavelength dependence displayed by the carbon nanodots. The contour plot shows that the emission is approximately centered around 500nm. These emission characteristics were very similar and reproducible for the many different batches of carbon nanodots produced by our method. In addition, the emission spectra were similar, if not identical, for all the different fractions of nanodots collected by centrifugation. Subsequently, centrifugation was effective at removing the larger sized particles (as seen visually) but has little to no effect in separating out the smaller sized particles, which are fractions observed to have the emission.

By taking the well utilized fluorophore, quinine sulfate, and using it to determine the relative quantum yield of the carbon nanodots, we find the quantum yield of these carbon nanodots to be <2%. When comparing over different centrifugation fractions, the quantum yields were all similar, <2%. However, the higher spin speeds (14500rpm) were consistently slightly higher (∼2%) than the lower spin speeds, i.e. <3000rpm (∼1–1.5%).

Time resolved experiments revealed multi-exponential luminescence decay for carbon nanodots. The decay and fitting traces, along with max entropy results can be seen in Figure 3 . The averaged lifetime for all centrifugation fractions collected was approximately 4ns, when excited with a 474nm NanoLED, the emission collected at 530nm. Several other experiments were conducted to see if lifetimes were affected by changing either excitation or emission wavelengths. Those results are displayed in the supporting material and reveal that there are slight changes in the average lifetime when those parameters are changed.

The nature of the fluorescence emission observed from c-dots is not fully understood today. Some experimental data supports the picture that emissive centers, based on polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), are formed during the combustion process and that these centers are distributed throughout an amorphous carbon framework. Since pure PAHs show a wide range of fluorescence decays times, e.g. see insert to Figure 3B, it is not surprising that time-resolved decays recorded for c-dots in the present work shows multiexponential photophysics, and requires three exponential terms for an adequate fit, i.e. k =3 in Eq. (1). To gain further insight into the photophysics we analyzed data in terms of a distribution function using the maximum entropy method. For better stability of the fitting algorithm 105 counts were collected at the peak of the fluorescence decay, see Figure 3A and the recovered distribution in Figure 3B. Although the data can be adequately described by a three exponential decay function, with lifetimes 600ps, 2ns and 6ns, respectively, it is evident from the life-time distribution that the photophysics is far more complex, and underpinned by a broad range of decay times, tailing up to 25ns. The chemical structures of some common PAHs are shown in Figure 3B. These PAHs show monoexponential decays in non-polar solvents, ranging from 4.3ns for Perylene in methanol to 122ns for Pyrene in cyclohexane. As a comparison to the c-dots investigated in this work, the maximum entropy lifetime distribution obtained for Perylene is also shown in Figure 3B, and further supports the view that the c-dot emission is underpinned by a broad range of emissive states.

Metal-enhanced fluorescence (plasmonic) experiments were additionally undertaken to ascertain whether the nanodots’ emission could be enhanced by the presence of near-field surface plasmons [4–9,16–18], given their low free-space quantum yields. These experiments were carried out in 96-well plates containing some wells with a plasmonic silver coating and some uncoated wells (regular plastic), subsequently acting as a control sample. The coating of silver was undertaken as previously described [17]. Figure 4 shows the enhanced emission of the carbon nanodots to be about 30 times at lambda max, when excited with 482nm (Varian) light, when comparing silver coated wells to uncoated wells (control sample). MEF was also studied at several other wavelengths on the Varian fluorometer (320 and 340nm) and with two additional laser sources (473 and 532nm). The emission enhancements for those wavelengths on coated wells (data not shown) were approximately 10–20 times greater than the control sample, depending on the excitation source, as compared to uncoated wells. The wavelength dependence of carbon nanodot MEF is shown in Figure 4, a property of the enhancing silver surface, and was as expected [5].

In this Letter, we have investigated both the photophysical and the plasmonic enhanced photophysical properties of combustion synthesized carbon nanodots. These carbon nanodots have a unique wavelength dependent emission, a broad absorption and a complex and inhomogeneous grouping of emissive lifetimes. The free-space quantum yield of the nanodots was ‘fraction dependent’, and all low, typically <2%. However, in the presence of silver plasmonic surfaces, the emission was remarkably enhanced, as much as 30 times (3000%). Given that the carbon nanodots described here only become highly luminescent close-to the MEF surfaces, then our findings suggest that carbon nanodots, would make excellent luminescence labels for either DNA/RNA or antibody based assays. Only when the assay is complete, will the carbon nanodot emission be significantly enhanced near-to the plasmonic surface, Supplemental Figure S1. Given that the MEF effect is well-known to be a near-field effect, enhancing labels within 50nm from the surface [18], then our findings support the notion of using carbon nanodots in washless immunoassays; i.e. only carbon dots close to the surface are enhanced, dots distal from the metal are not enhanced, and due to their low free space quantum yields, <2%, are unlikely to provide a high background emission in an immunoassay, Supplemental Figure S2. Further work is underway in our laboratory in this regard and will be published in due course.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Institute of Fluorescence and University of Maryland, Baltimore County for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cplett.2015.01.035 [19].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
